name: Old World Reset
description: The 'Old World Reset' theory posits that a cataclysmic event, or series of events, led to a significant disruption in global civilization, resulting in the destruction or concealment of advanced technologies, historical records, and cultural achievements of a previous, possibly more sophisticated, society. Proponents believe this reset occurred sometime in the relatively recent past, often citing the 18th or 19th century. Evidence cited includes architectural anomalies like 'orphanages' being larger than needed, suggesting a population loss; the prevalence of mud flood theories suggesting widespread inundation; and 'Tartarian' maps indicating a lost empire. Symmetrical architecture, seemingly advanced engineering feats in older buildings, and inconsistencies in historical narratives are also presented as supporting evidence. Mainstream historians and scientists largely dismiss the Old World Reset as a conspiracy theory lacking credible evidence. They attribute architectural styles to evolving construction techniques, historical inconsistencies to errors in record-keeping or propaganda, and 'mud floods' to localized flooding or geological events. The theory's appeal lies in its potential to explain unexplained historical events and to offer an alternative narrative to mainstream history.
subtitle: Cataclysmic Civilization Reset Conspiracy Theory

claims.claim: A global cataclysm (mud flood, great fire, etc.) caused widespread destruction in the 18th/19th century.
claims.evidence: Reports of widespread flooding, fires, and building damage during that period; mud-covered lower levels of old buildings; architectural inconsistencies.
claims.criticism: Localized natural disasters and urban fires were common occurrences. 'Mud floods' are often misidentified geological events. No widespread, globally consistent evidence supports a cataclysm of this scale.

claims.claim: Evidence of a lost, advanced civilization (Tartaria) was erased from history.
claims.evidence: References to Tartaria on old maps; ornate and seemingly advanced architecture in older buildings; claims of suppressed technologies.
claims.criticism: Tartaria was a historical region vaguely defined on maps; ornate architecture reflects evolving styles and available technology; alleged suppressed technologies are often misinterpreted or based on unsubstantiated claims.

claims.claim: Existing historical narratives are deliberately falsified to conceal the 'reset' and the lost civilization.
claims.evidence: Inconsistencies in historical accounts; perceived 'gaps' in the historical record; claims of fabricated historical figures or events.
claims.criticism: Historical accounts are subject to interpretation and bias, but deliberate falsification on a global scale is highly improbable. Gaps in the record are common due to loss or destruction of documents over time.

keyFigures.name: Anatoly Fomenko
keyFigures.role: Promoter of New Chronology, a related theory questioning conventional history

keyFigures.name: Philipp Druzhinin
keyFigures.role: YouTuber and proponent of the Tartarian theory

keyFigures.name: Jason Colavito
keyFigures.role: Researcher and critic of pseudohistory and alternative archaeology

timeline.event: Anatoly Fomenko begins publishing his 'New Chronology' theory.
The 'Tartaria' and 'Old World Reset' theories gain traction online through alternative history communities.
Continued online discussion and proliferation of related conspiracy theories.

sources.title: Jason Colavito
